title: Commentary by Chapter
description: An expository commentary on the Epistle of James, developed from the sermon series by Pastor Christian Nartatez at EIC Rueil-Malmaison.
disclaimer: This study resource was developed by the pastoral team at EIC Rueil-Malmaison, drawing directly from our chapter-by-chapter sermon series on the Epistle of James. Every definition, explanation, and application has been carefully prepared to serve the spiritual growth of our D-Group and the wider body of Christ. This is not an academic commentary. It is a pastoral tool, written with the prayer that it will help you read, understand, and live the message that James delivered to the scattered believers of his generation and to us today.

1.chapter_title: Faith, Provision and Silence in the Christian Life

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Beloved brothers and sisters, there is a question that every honest Christian must face at some point in their walk with the Lord: Is my faith real? Not real in the sense of whether I believe certain doctrines to be true, because even the demons believe and they tremble. The question goes deeper than that. Is my faith the kind that changes the way I respond when the phone rings at two in the morning with bad news? Is it the kind that shapes how I treat the person who has nothing to offer me in return? Is it the kind that holds me steady when everything around me is falling apart? The writer of this letter understood that question better than most. He calls himself simply 'James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ.' The evidence of history and Scripture points to James the brother of Jesus, a man who grew up in the same house as the Messiah and yet did not believe in Him during His earthly ministry. It was only after the resurrection, when the risen Christ appeared to him personally, that everything changed. The skeptic became a servant. The brother became a disciple. And the man who once doubted wrote a letter so practical, so direct, and so uncompromising that Martin Luther himself struggled with it. James writes to the twelve tribes scattered abroad, to Jewish believers dispersed throughout the Roman world, and his message is devastatingly simple: if your faith is real, it will show. This first chapter lays the foundation for everything that follows. It moves through three great themes: faith that endures through trials and receives wisdom from God, the provision of a Father who gives every good gift, and the discipline of learning to listen before we speak and to live what we hear.

sections.title: Faith, Patience and Wisdom: The Science of Believing (James 1:1-12)
sections.content: James wastes no time with pleasantries. His very first instruction is one of the most counterintuitive commands in all of Scripture: count it all joy when you fall into various trials. Not if, but when. And not some joy, but all joy. How is this possible? Because faith has its own science, its own logic that operates differently from the logic of this world. The worldly mind says: avoid pain at all costs. The mind of faith says: this pain has a purpose, and the God who allowed it is trustworthy. The testing of your faith, James says, produces endurance. And endurance must have its complete work so that you may be perfect and complete, lacking in nothing. Think about that for a moment. The word 'perfect' here does not mean sinless. In the original language it carries the sense of being fully developed, fully mature, having reached the goal for which you were designed. And the only way to reach that maturity is through the furnace of trial. It is very possible that the heat of the furnace feels as if it will melt you entirely, but the Lord only allows it to reach the exact temperature needed to refine you and burn away the impurities. The trial is less than a just punishment. It is a means of correction and spiritual education without which it is impossible for us to reach any degree of greatness in the kingdom of God. Now, if the trial produces endurance, the trial also drives us to our knees in prayer. And this is where wisdom enters the picture. James says: if any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask of God, who gives to all generously and without reproach. The Christian never needs wisdom more than when they are in the middle of a trial, because the outcome depends on whether you bear it correctly, according to the will of God and until the time appointed by the Father. We need wisdom so that we choose the true path without turning to the right or to the left. We need wisdom so that we understand what God wants us to learn when He denies us something we desperately wanted. We need wisdom when He places a heavy burden on our shoulders that we did not ask for. But there is a condition: ask in faith, without doubting. The one who doubts is like a wave of the sea, thrown back and forth by the wind. James calls this person 'double minded,' a word that in the original language literally means 'two souled.' This person is unstable in everything, not just in prayer but in all of life. The heroism of faith, as one theologian beautifully wrote, manifests itself in two ways: it is both suffering and militant. It endures and it fights. And the one who endures to the end will receive the crown of life that the Lord has promised to those who love Him.

sections.title: Provision and Sustenance: The Father of Lights (James 1:13-18)
sections.content: After establishing the purpose of trials, James now addresses a dangerous lie that some were telling themselves: 'I am tempted by God.' Let no one say that, James responds, because God cannot be tempted by evil, and He Himself tempts no one. Each person is tempted when they are dragged away by their own desire and enticed. Then desire, when it has conceived, gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown produces death. Notice the progression: desire, conception, birth, growth, death. Sin operates like a pregnancy. It starts invisible, grows in secret, and eventually produces something that cannot be hidden. James is telling us: do not blame God for your failures. The enemy is closer than you think. It lives inside your own heart. But then, in one of the most beautiful transitions in all of Scripture, James pivots from the darkness of temptation to the brilliance of divine generosity. 'Do not be deceived, my beloved brothers. Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom can be no variation nor turning shadow.' God is the source of everything good in your life. He is not subject to the laws of the market. He does not respond to any earthly government. He does not need to check the exchange rate or follow the trends of the moment. If an earthly investor takes care of his enterprise, will not the Father of lights take infinitely greater care of His children? The detail is this: the Lord is more interested in your sanctification and your spiritual growth than in your economic prosperity. That is the reality. And that is why James adds: 'Of His own will He gave birth to us by the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits of His creatures.' He called us to be firstfruits. That word implies position, recognition, and preservation. If you are His child, you can be certain that whatever happens, the Lord will take care of you. That does not mean you will never suffer, or feel pain, or experience moments of great need. God does not raise spoiled and entitled children. In the moments of hardship, disappointment, and suffering, He is often dealing with the unfinished business of our hearts, those things we have not yet talked about with Him, those areas we have not yet surrendered. The trial, the difficulty, the tribulation is often the workshop where the Lord is sculpting us, removing the excess material of sin, unbelief, and the habits that want to stop our spiritual growth.

sections.title: Attitudes, Communication and Living the Word (James 1:19-27)
sections.content: James now gives us three commands that, if we actually obeyed them, would transform every relationship in our lives: be swift to hear, slow to speak, and slow to anger. The order matters. First listen. Then speak. And let anger be the very last thing you reach for. The anger of man does not produce the righteousness of God. How many arguments could have been avoided if we had simply listened before we opened our mouths? How many friendships could have been saved if we had been slow to react instead of quick to explode? A wise man once said: 'Blessed is the man whose mouth is in his heart and whose heart is not in his mouth. The one is wisdom; the other is foolishness.' We should speak the Word more to our own hearts and avoid putting our hearts into our reckless speaking, because the heart of man is deceitful above all things. It will not be our zeal for the Word that illuminates the understanding of those who are deceived. It will not be our intellect or our power of persuasion that converts the heart. Only the power of the Spirit can save. But the way we express ourselves can determine whether people will listen to us at all. Then James introduces one of his most famous illustrations: the mirror. If anyone is a hearer of the word and not a doer, he is like a man who looks at his natural face in a mirror, sees the mess, and walks away without fixing anything. In the first century, mirrors were not made of glass but of polished metal. The image was real but imperfect. When you hear the Word of God, it shows you who you truly are and who you ought to be. But if you hear and do nothing, you remain exactly as you were, and the hearing served no purpose at all. The true internal hearing, as one scholar wrote, is always receiving again the word that has already been implanted within us as the seed of regeneration. What good would the preached word be unless it had hidden points of contact in the hearts and consciences of believers? The hearer who forgets corresponds exactly to the second type of soil in the parable of the Sower. Finally, James closes this chapter with a definition of true religion that is breathtaking in its simplicity: visit orphans and widows in their affliction, and keep yourself unstained by the world. That is it. Not buildings, not programs, not impressive liturgies. Visit the people nobody visits. Help the people nobody helps. And guard your heart from the contamination of a world that wants to reshape you in its image. This is the religion that is pure and undefiled before God the Father.

sections.title: A Final Word
sections.content: Brothers and sisters, this first chapter of the Epistle of James is a treasury of practical wisdom anchored in the unchanging character of God. If we consider our faith, then inevitably we must recognize that it is an active faith, one that is dynamic, living, and in constant movement, responding and growing in our relationship with the Lord. If our faith grows in dependence on Him, it will only be through the trial and the tribulation. That same tribulation will exercise our patience, and in it we will find good soil to acquire and learn wisdom that is essential for the Christian life. That wisdom will give us discernment to understand that God is the Father of lights from whom comes every good gift and every perfect gift. He is our provision. He is our sustenance. And in Him there is no variation, no shadow of change. May the Lord preserve us in His faith, provide for us in His generosity, and teach us to live in silence and with gratitude the Christian life that He has called us to live.

2.chapter_title: Love and Works in the Faith

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: In his autobiography, Mahatma Gandhi wrote that during his student days he read the Gospels seriously and considered becoming a Christian. He believed that in Jesus he could find the solution to the caste system that divided the people of India. One Sunday he decided to attend a church service and speak with the minister about converting. But when he entered the sanctuary, the usher refused to give him a seat and suggested he go worship with his own people. Gandhi left that church and never returned. 'If Christians also have differences of caste,' he said, 'I might as well remain a Hindu.' Whether this story is entirely accurate in every detail, the principle it illustrates is devastating. The world is watching how we treat people, and when our practice contradicts our profession, we become the greatest obstacle to the very Gospel we claim to believe. James understood this danger. In this second chapter, he attacks two of the most destructive diseases that can infect a local church: the sin of showing favoritism to the wealthy while despising the poor, and the deadly illusion of a faith that talks but never acts. He does so with the intensity of a man who has no time for religious games and no patience for comfortable Christianity.

sections.title: The Faith that Loves Without Partiality (James 2:1-13)
sections.content: James opens this chapter with a command that cuts straight to the bone: do not hold the faith of our glorious Lord Jesus Christ with partiality. The grammar here is important. He is not making a suggestion. He is identifying something that was already happening in these churches and telling them to stop. The hypothetical scenario he paints is vivid. A man walks into the assembly wearing a gold ring and fine clothing, and immediately the ushers give him the best seat. Then a poor man comes in with dirty clothes, and they tell him to stand over there, or sit on the floor by my footstool. Have you not made distinctions among yourselves, James asks, and become judges with evil thoughts? In the ancient Jewish tradition, when an accused person came before a judge, it was customary according to the law that they should not lift their face before the judge. This way, if the accused happened to be a relative of the judge, or someone wealthy and influential, or a close friend, the judge would not be able to recognize them and show favoritism. James is applying that same principle to the church: do not lift the face of anyone because of their appearance, their wealth, or their social status. As Paul teaches in Galatians: there is neither Jew nor Greek, neither slave nor free, neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus. Everything that men have built to divide and segregate one another, God has torn down in Christ. As one preacher once said, the ground is level at the foot of the cross. Then James delivers a devastating blow: did not God choose the poor of this world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom? The poor believe. The Gospel is preached to them. And yet in many churches today, the pattern of favoritism continues under different names. Those who give the largest offerings get the best treatment. Those who have influence get special access. Those who cannot contribute financially are quietly pushed to the margins. James reminds us that showing partiality is not just poor etiquette. It is sin. And the royal law of Scripture commands us to love our neighbor as ourselves. If you keep the whole law but stumble in this one point, you are guilty of all of it. But then James offers one of the most beautiful statements in all of Scripture: mercy triumphs over judgment. If God dealt with us strictly by the rules, none of us would survive. But mercy steps in, looks at what we deserve, and chooses to give us something infinitely better. We are called to extend that same mercy to every person who walks through our doors.

sections.title: The Faith that Works Without Excuses (James 2:14-20)
sections.content: Now James enters the passage that has generated more theological debate than perhaps any other in the New Testament: the relationship between faith and works. What good is it, he asks, if someone says he has faith but has no works? Can that faith save him? Before we go any further, let us be clear about something that many people misunderstand. James and Paul are not contradicting each other. They are teaching two sides of the same coin. They are showing two wings of the same bird. They speak to two different groups with two different problems using the same words. Paul was dealing with people who said that works of the law must be added to faith in order to earn God's favor. Paul answered that error by declaring that salvation is by faith alone, apart from works of the law. But Paul also taught that saving faith is not dead; it is alive and works through love. James, on the other hand, was dealing with people who said that since they had faith, they did not need to demonstrate love through a life of obedience. James answered that error by teaching that faith which produces no evidence of life is not real faith at all. Both James and Paul teach that salvation is by faith alone, and both teach that genuine faith is never alone; it always manifests itself through acts of love that express the believer's gratitude to God. James then paints another hypothetical scenario to drive his point home. A brother or sister comes to you without clothing and without food. And you say to them: 'Go in peace, be warm and be filled,' but you give them nothing they actually need. What good is that? If your theology consists of sending people away with pious words while your wallet is full and your pantry is stocked, then examine your profession of faith carefully. On the day of judgment, the Lord will not ask who made the prayer of faith, or who was under the coverage of the most anointed apostle, or who had the most impressive theological degrees. He will ask: I was hungry, and did you feed me? I was thirsty, and did you give me water? I was a stranger, and did you welcome me? I was sick, and did you visit me? Even the demons believe that God is one, and they tremble. They have a theology far more complete than ours. They know God intimately. And yet in all their knowledge they have rejected Him, turned their backs on Him, and been condemned for their rebellion.

sections.title: The Faith that is Proven: Abraham and Rahab (James 2:21-26)
sections.content: What James does next is very radical, very bold, and deeply intentional, especially considering his audience. He takes two characters from the Old Testament and places them side by side as examples of living faith: Abraham and Rahab. Think about the contrast. Abraham is the father of the faith, the patriarch, the man who believed God and went out in obedience. God raised from him a nation from which the Messiah would come. He was deeply respected in Jewish tradition, honored above almost all others. And Rahab? A prostitute. A Canaanite woman. A foreigner with no standing, no reputation, and no merit. And yet both of them appear in the genealogy of the Lord Jesus Christ. What does this teach us? First, that God was not partial in giving the gift of faith. He chose by grace both one and the other. Second, both participated in the expansion of God's plan. Third, both proved their faith through actions and deeds. Abraham believed the promise and waited in faith, and then his faith was manifested when he climbed the mountain to offer his son Isaac. He had to have already killed Isaac in his heart before God stopped his hand. Rahab believed the promise that she would be adopted into the family of Israel regardless of her profession, her ethnicity, and her past, and her faith was proven when she risked her life and the life of her family to hide the spies at Jericho. James closes this argument with an illustration that leaves no room for misunderstanding: as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead. A body lying on a table that does not breathe, does not move, does not respond to anything, that body is not sleeping. It is dead. In the exact same way, a faith that confesses Christ on Sunday but lives as if He does not exist from Monday to Saturday is not a weak faith. It is a dead faith. When we have truly come to know Jesus in a living, personal relationship and not merely in the intellect, our entire life, our thoughts, and our actions must change. We will not become perfect. We will not stop sinning entirely. But evidently we will not be the same persons we were before. Our life will no longer revolve around the world but around the Lord.

sections.title: A Final Word
sections.content: Brothers and sisters, this chapter confronts us with two of the most uncomfortable questions we can face. The first is: do I treat people differently based on what they can do for me? And the second is: does my daily life provide any visible evidence that my faith is real? James is not trying to make us feel guilty. He is trying to wake us up. The faith that loves without partiality and the faith that works without excuses are not two different kinds of faith. They are the same living faith expressed in two directions: toward the people around us and toward the God who saved us. May the Lord give us the grace to examine our hearts honestly, to repent where we have failed, and to live the kind of faith that Abraham and Rahab demonstrated, a faith that does not merely confess with the mouth but confirms with the hands, the feet, and the open doors of our lives.

3.chapter_title: The Tongue: Teachers, Fire and Wisdom

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: There is an old story about a woman who once approached one of the Puritan ministers in London and told him that the scarves he wore with his pulpit suit were too long and that they bothered her enormously. She asked his permission to shorten them. Graciously, he consented, and the woman, who had come prepared with scissors, cut the scarves to her liking and returned the pieces to the minister. When he received them, he thanked her and said: 'Now, my good woman, there is something on you that is too long and that has bothered me greatly, and since one good turn deserves another, I would like permission to shorten it.' 'Certainly,' said the woman, 'you have my permission, and here are the scissors.' To which the minister replied: 'Very well, madam, please stick out your tongue.' It is a humorous story, but it carries a truth that is anything but funny. The tongue is the most powerful and the most dangerous member of the human body. Through the analysis of the tongue, a doctor can diagnose the diseases of the body, a philosopher can diagnose the diseases of the mind, and the Christian can diagnose the diseases of the soul. James dedicates this entire chapter to two closely related subjects: the enormous responsibility of those who teach the Word of God, and the terrifying power of the tongue that every human being carries in their mouth. These are not separate topics. They are deeply connected, because the teacher is the person whose tongue carries the greatest weight and the greatest potential for both healing and destruction.

sections.title: The Responsibility of Teachers and the Power of Words (James 3:1-6)
sections.content: James opens with a warning that should make every pastor, elder, and Sunday school teacher pause and reflect: let not many of you become teachers, my brothers, knowing that we will receive a stricter judgment. The word he uses for 'become' in the original language does not simply mean 'to be.' It carries the idea of constituting yourself, of setting yourself up in a position. James is not discouraging the gift of teaching. He is warning against the rush to occupy a position of authority without understanding the weight of responsibility that comes with it. In the Jewish tradition, a rabbi was treated with the highest respect. The obligations toward a rabbi were said to exceed even the obligations toward a father, because parents give you existence in this world, but a rabbi gives you existence in the world to come. It was tremendously easy for a rabbi to become the kind of person Jesus described: a spiritual tyrant, a trafficker in piety, someone in love with distinctions and public respect. The same danger exists today. There are teachers who twist the doctrine of grace to suit their own agendas. There are pastors whose private lives contradict every word they preach from the pulpit. There are leaders who were placed in authority before they were spiritually ready, and the result is always the same: false doctrines entering the church, problems of conduct, either excessive or absent discipline, divisions over authority, and congregations that slowly transform into something that looks more like a cult than a family of believers. Then James moves to the tongue itself, and his illustrations are unforgettable. A small bit controls the mouth of a powerful horse and directs the entire animal. A small rudder steers a massive ship through fierce winds. In the same way, the tongue is a small member but it boasts of great things. And then the image becomes terrifying: see how a small fire can spread to a large forest. The tongue is a fire. It is a world of iniquity set among our members, defiling the whole body and setting the entire course of life on fire, and it is itself set on fire by the very pit of hell. This is the only place outside of the words of Jesus where this particular word for hell appears in the New Testament. James is telling us that the destructive power of reckless speech has a diabolical origin. When we give free rein to our tongues, we are not merely being careless. We are allowing an instrument of hell to operate through us.

sections.title: The Untamable Evil and the Dignity of Every Person (James 3:7-12)
sections.content: James now makes a statement that, at first glance, seems to offer no hope at all: every kind of animal, bird, reptile, and sea creature has been tamed by human beings, but no human being can tame the tongue. It is a restless evil, full of deadly poison. With it we bless our God and Father, and with it we curse people who are made in the image of God. Out of the same mouth come blessing and cursing. My brothers, these things ought not to be so. The sharp and cutting tongue is the only tool that becomes sharper the more it is used. And many of us have reason to repent more of the things we have said than of the things we kept to ourselves. But if no one can tame the tongue, does that mean we are hopeless? Not at all. Just as the horse needs the rider and the ship needs the pilot, our tongue needs the Lord to control it. A tongue that is under the governance of the Holy Spirit is evidence that the one directing and controlling is God Himself, not our flesh. There is also a profound point about human dignity embedded in this passage. James says we curse people who are made in the image of God. God made every human being in His image and likeness. He never did this with any angel. Christ Jesus did not die for angels. He died for people, for the ones chosen by God for salvation, for His people. Should this not tell us something about the value and respect we owe to every person? Each human being, regardless of whether they deserve our kindness or not, regardless of whether they are Christian or not, was made in the image of God. That image has been damaged and scarred by sin, but it is still there. That is the argument James is making: you are not better than anyone else when you insult, mistreat, or speak all kinds of evil against another person. Then James uses nature to drive the point home. Does a spring pour out fresh water and bitter water from the same opening? Can a fig tree produce olives, or a vine produce figs? Of course not. And in the same way, no spring should produce both salt water and fresh water. The hypocrisy of using the same mouth to sing hymns to God on Sunday and to curse our neighbor on Monday reveals that something at the very root of our lives has not been truly surrendered to the Lord.

sections.title: Two Kinds of Wisdom: From Below and From Above (James 3:13-18)
sections.content: James closes this chapter by painting a vivid contrast between two kinds of wisdom. The first is earthly, unspiritual, and demonic. Where there is bitter jealousy and selfish ambition, there is disorder and every kind of evil practice. This is the wisdom that builds platforms instead of people, that turns congregations into franchises, and that measures success by numbers, fame, and influence rather than by faithfulness to the Word. It is very sad to observe how many pastors look down on other pastors because of the university where they studied, or the size of the church they lead, or the liturgical tradition they follow, or how famous they are. There are pastors who never returned to a fellowship of ministers because of the shallowness, arrogance, and condescension they encountered. Pastors of large congregations tend to treat pastors of small churches differently. If the highest standard for many leaders is not the Bible itself, then anything can take its place: the fame of this one, the anointing of that one, the reputation of another, the number of members, the size of the buildings, the trends of the moment. But the wisdom that comes from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, reasonable, full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality and without hypocrisy. Notice that purity comes first. Before you worry about being peaceful or gentle, make sure that your heart is clean before God. And then the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace. A true biblical teacher and pastor is one who is called by the Lord, whose calling is confirmed by the sheep who follow. A true pastor smells like sheep, because he has been among them. A true pastor feeds the flock, not counts the flock, because what matters is not how many are present but how well they are being nourished. A true pastor protects the sheep from wolves and disciplines the sheep who wander. A true pastor has two voices: one for calling the sheep, and another for scaring away the wolves. And a true pastor uses the shepherd's staff, not a pillow. The staff is used to pull the wandering sheep back by the neck, and to give a little tap on the leg to the one that is jumping around too much. These are the marks of the biblical shepherd.

sections.title: A Final Word
sections.content: Brothers and sisters, this chapter confronts every one of us. Whether you teach or not, you carry a tongue in your mouth, and that tongue has the power to build up or to tear down, to heal or to wound, to bless or to curse. James is not telling us to stop talking. He is telling us to surrender our speech to the Lord, to let the Holy Spirit be the pilot of the ship and the rider of the horse. When the urge to speak is strongest, when the temptation to lash out is most intense, when we feel that we simply cannot hold it in any longer, that is precisely the moment when we must ask the Lord for patience and wisdom to remain silent. It is not about being quiet when it is convenient. It is about exercising self-control when every part of our flesh is screaming to speak. May the Lord make our tongues instruments of peace, of truth, and of the kind of wisdom that comes from above, the wisdom that is pure, peaceable, gentle, full of mercy, and without hypocrisy.

4.chapter_title: The World, Humility and the Will of God

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: When the apostle Paul wrote to the Christians in Philippi, he knew how important it was for them to understand the concept of living as citizens of one place while physically residing in another. Philippi was a Roman colony, a model city that replicated Roman customs, Roman architecture, Roman clothing, and the Latin language, even though it was nearly a thousand miles from Rome. If you walked into the city, you would feel as if you had entered an Italian suburb of the capital itself. So when Paul told those believers, 'Our citizenship is in heaven,' they understood exactly what he meant. But there is an important difference between the Christian calling and the human tendency to make ourselves at home in foreign territory. The difference is that no matter how well we adapt, no matter how creative our efforts, Christians must never forget that this world is not our home. There is a sense of displacement that must remain at the core of all our experiences. Adaptation may be second nature, but it can also be the death of our first nature, the nature created in the image of God and then recreated by His grace in Christ. This fourth chapter of James confronts us with the stark reality of that tension. Where do wars and conflicts come from? From our own pleasures that wage war inside us. What happens when we try to be friends with both God and the world? We become enemies of God. And what is the only path forward? Humility, submission, and an honest recognition that our life is a vapor that appears for a little while and then vanishes.

sections.title: The War Within and the Friendship that Kills (James 4:1-6)
sections.content: James opens this chapter with a question that pierces straight to the root of human conflict: where do wars and fights among you come from? Do they not come from your pleasures that war in your members? Every day, in every moment, we fight an internal battle between the Spirit and the flesh that rebels. It is a constant struggle to die to ourselves and live for Christ. Success, fame, and riches are always around the corner, and all it takes is for us to shift our gaze and change our direction. But what happens when the things we do not have become the center of our lives instead of Christ? What are we willing to do for comfort, for luxury, for influence and power? You covet and do not have. You murder and are consumed with envy but cannot obtain. You fight and make war. And yet you do not have, because you do not ask. And when you do ask, you do not receive, because you ask with wrong motives, so that you may spend it on your pleasures. James is describing prayers that are aimed at the wrong target. We must learn to value patience, the joy of salvation, and the countless blessings of the Christian life that have nothing to do with material possessions. Can we ask in moments of need, not only for material provision but also for the comfort to endure the circumstance? Can we ask for peace to continue day after day without losing hope? Can we ask for joy when our season is one of sadness and affliction? Can we ask for grace when we are being stung by sin and our own wickedness? A survivor of the Nazi concentration camps, who lost his entire family in the Second World War, once said: 'They can take almost everything from a man, but they cannot take away what he chooses to do with the circumstances he faces.' Then James delivers one of the most sobering warnings in all of Scripture: you adulterers and adulteresses, do you not know that friendship with the world is hostility toward God? Whoever wants to be a friend of the world makes himself an enemy of God. As Spurgeon once said: 'If I had a brother who had been murdered, what would you think of me if I daily kept company with the assassin who drove the dagger into my brother's heart? Sin murdered Christ; will you be a friend to it?' But God gives more grace. He resists the proud, but He gives grace to the humble. The proud get opposition. The humble get power.

sections.title: Humility, Grace and Drawing Near to God (James 4:7-10)
sections.content: James now gives us a series of commands that read like marching orders for the soldier of Christ. Submit to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you. Draw near to God, and He will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, you sinners. Purify your hearts, you double minded. The way we resist the devil is not through spiritual warfare rituals, or by rebuking him at every corner, or by performing mystical ceremonies and attaching Christian symbols to everything we own. The way we resist the devil is this: in the evil day, in the crucial moment of trial and affliction, we fall on our faces and worship the Lord. We embrace the will of God, exalting and glorifying His name because He is worthy, regardless of what is happening around us, even if it costs us our lives. Remember the three young men in Babylon: 'Our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace. But even if He does not, let it be known to you, O king, that we will not serve your gods or worship the golden image.' And remember Job, who after losing everything he had, stood up, tore his robe, shaved his head, and fell to the ground in worship, saying: 'The Lord gave, and the Lord has taken away. Blessed be the name of the Lord.' The devil is a proud and arrogant being. He hates seeing believers take the posture of Job. That is what resistance looks like: not shouting at the darkness, but worshiping in the middle of it. According to the context of what James has been teaching, how do we draw near to God and purify our hearts? By combating our flesh in order to live for the glory of God. By abandoning the craving for the comfortable and pleasurable life that the world offers. By using our hands to serve the church of Christ and not merely our own interests. Our hearts are purified when we meditate on the virtue of humility, when we can humble ourselves without complaining and give thanks for the little or the much that we have. Lament, mourn, and weep, James says. Our pain, our tears, and our lament are a pleasing aroma before the Lord when we bring them to Him in surrender. He does not forbid us from crying. He simply asks that we do it in His presence, trusting Him and waiting on Him for our rescue and consolation.

sections.title: The Danger of Judging and the Brevity of Life (James 4:11-17)
sections.content: James now addresses two final issues that reveal the arrogance of the human heart: speaking evil against one another and making plans as if we were in control of tomorrow. Do not speak against one another, brothers. He who speaks against a brother and judges his brother speaks against the law and judges the law. But if you judge the law, you are not a doer of the law but a judge. There is only one Lawgiver and Judge, the one who is able to save and to destroy. But who are you to judge another? There is an old story about Charles Spurgeon and Joseph Parker, who both had churches in London in the nineteenth century. Parker once commented on the condition of children admitted to Spurgeon's orphanage, and it was reported to Spurgeon that Parker had criticized the orphanage itself. Spurgeon attacked Parker from the pulpit the following Sunday. The newspapers printed the story and it became the talk of the town. People flocked to Parker's church to hear his response. Instead of retaliating, Parker said: 'I understand Dr. Spurgeon is not in his pulpit today, and this is the Sunday they take an offering for the orphanage. I suggest we take a love offering here instead.' The crowd was delighted. The ushers had to empty the collection plates three times. Later that week, Spurgeon knocked on Parker's door. 'You have practiced grace on me,' he said. 'You have given me not what I deserved but what I needed.' That is the spirit James is calling for. Criticism based on personal grudges, envy, or selfish interests is not discernment. It is sin. Then James turns to the brevity of life. Come now, you who say, 'Today or tomorrow we will go into this city and spend a year there and trade and make a profit.' Yet you do not know what your life will be like tomorrow. You are a vapor that appears for a little time and then vanishes. Instead, you ought to say, 'If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that.' The doctrine of Divine Providence affirms that God is in absolute control of all things. This doctrine stands in direct opposition to the idea that the universe is governed by chance. The believer is not an arrogant person who rebels against the purposes of God. And then the chapter closes with a statement that haunts every comfortable Christian: to the one who knows the right thing to do and does not do it, to him it is sin.

sections.title: A Final Word
sections.content: Brothers and sisters, this chapter calls us to a radical honesty about our lives. Are we fighting wars that come from our own selfish desires? Are we trying to maintain a friendship with the world while claiming to love God? Are we making plans for tomorrow as if we were the ones in control? James is not condemning ambition or planning. He is condemning the arrogance that forgets God in the process. May we learn to hold our plans with open hands, to humble ourselves before the Lord so that He may lift us up in His timing, and to live every day with the sober awareness that our life is a vapor, and that every moment is an opportunity to glorify the one who gave it to us.

5.chapter_title: Resources, Attitude and Will in Christ

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: There is a striking image that captures the state of the world without Christ. The motors of a clock, separated from their pendulum, were left sitting in a living room. The seconds ticked away frantically. Without the pendulum, the entire intricate mechanism rattled in a blind rush, going nowhere. That is a picture of the world today. God created all things to move in proper balance, but humanity has rejected the Lord Jesus Christ, who is the Providence of God. People speak of Providence as if it were a force, but Scripture says it is a Person: Christ, who sustains all things by the word of His power. Nothing will move to perfection until He returns. This fifth and final chapter of James brings together the great themes of the letter in a powerful crescendo. It addresses the wealthy who have oppressed their workers and hoarded their riches. It calls believers to patient endurance as they wait for the coming of the Lord. And it closes with some of the most practical instructions on prayer, confession, and the restoration of the wanderer that we find anywhere in the New Testament. James begins with thunder and ends with tenderness, and the thread that holds it all together is the unshakable reality that the Judge is standing at the door.

sections.title: A Warning to the Oppressors and the Stewardship of Resources (James 5:1-6)
sections.content: James opens this chapter with words that should make every comfortable person sit up and pay attention: come now, you rich, weep and howl for the miseries that are coming upon you. Your riches are corrupted. Your garments are moth eaten. Your gold and silver are corroded, and their corrosion will be a testimony against you and will eat your flesh like fire. You have stored up treasure in the last days. It is important to understand that James is not condemning wealth itself. The Bible recognizes both the pious poor and the pious rich. The widow of Zarephath was poor but faithful. Abraham, David, and Solomon were wealthy but devoted to God. Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus were rich men who served the Lord. What James condemns is not having wealth but abusing it, specifically in three terrible sins: the injustice of withholding wages from laborers, the hoarding of possessions that will testify against their owners on the day of judgment, and the extravagant living that fattens the heart while others go hungry. The word James uses for 'Lord' when he says 'the Lord of Armies' is remarkable. In the original language, this title speaks of God as the commander of the heavenly hosts, the one who has an army at His disposal that no earthly power can resist. This is the only time this title appears in the New Testament outside of a direct Old Testament quotation. James is pulling out the heaviest artillery to make his point: the cries of the cheated workers have reached the ears of the God who commands the armies of heaven. The material possessions of the world that are not used for the glory of God become nothing more than evidence of condemnation for those who hoard them. The question is not whether you are rich or poor, but what your resources are doing for the kingdom. Are you a steward who manages what belongs to God, or a proprietor who acts as if it all belongs to you?

sections.title: The Patient Farmer and the Coming of the Lord (James 5:7-12)
sections.content: After the thunder comes the tenderness. James now turns to the believers and says: be patient, therefore, brothers, until the coming of the Lord. And he gives us one of the most beautiful illustrations in the entire letter: the farmer who waits for the precious fruit of the earth, being patient over it until it receives the early and late rain. The farmer plants the seed and then waits. He does not dig up the seed every morning to see if it has sprouted. He trusts the process. He trusts the rain. He trusts the seasons. In the same way, we are called to establish our hearts, because the coming of the Lord is near. One scholar has estimated that there are 1,845 references to the second coming of Christ in the Old Testament, and 318 references in the 260 chapters of the New Testament, roughly one in every thirty verses. For every prophecy about the first coming of Christ, there are eight that point to His second coming. This is not a minor doctrine. It is one of the central pillars of the Christian hope. Then James addresses a problem that had developed among the suffering believers: they had begun to grumble and complain against one another. The oppression from the rich had created bitterness, and that bitterness had turned inward. James corrects them: do not grumble against one another, brothers, so that you may not be judged. The Judge is standing at the door. He hears everything. And we must not invade the prerogative of the one who alone has the right to render the final verdict. James points them to the prophets who spoke in the name of the Lord as examples of suffering and patience. He points them to Job, whose story demonstrates that God always has a purpose and a wonderful outcome in mind, even when we cannot see it. The Lord is full of compassion and mercy. Then James gives a final instruction about integrity in communication: do not swear, neither by heaven nor by earth nor by any other oath. Let your yes be yes and your no be no. The background is clear. There were people who could not keep their word. In the ancient world, as in ours, a person's reliability was measured by whether they did what they said they would do. James is saying: be the kind of person whose simple word is as binding as any oath. A true Christian community is one where everyone maintains their word as sacred, and where the mutual trust is so deep that oaths become unnecessary.

sections.title: Prayer, Confession and Restoring the Wanderer (James 5:13-20)
sections.content: James closes his letter with instructions so practical that they could serve as a handbook for any local church. Is anyone among you suffering? Let him pray. Is anyone cheerful? Let him sing praises. These are not suggestions. They are imperatives. The Christian life should be marked by a balanced and steady response to both good days and bad days, as prescribed here. In contrast with the complaints, criticisms, denunciations, and curses that James has been addressing throughout the letter, the believer's response to affliction should be prayer, and the response to joy should be praise. Is anyone sick? Let him call for the elders of the church, and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord. And the prayer of faith will save the one who is sick, and the Lord will raise him up. It is important to understand two dangerous extremes: the first says that sickness has no relationship to sin whatsoever, and the second says that sickness is always a direct product of sin. The truth is somewhere in the middle. James connects the healing of the body with the confession of sin and the prayer of the community. The oil served both as a medicinal remedy, since oil was commonly used for healing in the ancient world, and as a symbol of the care and attention of the church. The willingness of the sick person to call for the elders, to confess their condition of weakness, to ask for help, is itself an act of humility and surrender. Then James points us to Elijah, a man with a nature just like ours, who prayed earnestly that it would not rain, and it did not rain for three years and six months. And he prayed again, and the sky gave rain, and the earth produced its fruit. Elijah was not a superhero. He was a man who struggled with fear, with depression, with the desire to give up. After his greatest victory on Mount Carmel, he ran away from Jezebel and sat under a tree asking God to take his life. And yet this same fragile, imperfect man moved heaven and earth through prayer. If Elijah can do it, so can you. Finally, James closes with a word about restoring the one who has wandered from the truth. If anyone among you wanders from the truth and someone turns him back, let him know that he who turns a sinner from the error of his way will save a soul from death and will cover a multitude of sins. The letter that began with trials ends with restoration. The letter that opened with testing closes with rescue. This is the heart of God: not to leave His sheep wandering in the darkness, but to send someone after them to bring them home.

sections.title: A Final Word
sections.content: Brothers and sisters, the Epistle of James began with a call to count it all joy in the face of trials, and it ends with a call to pray, to confess, and to go after the one who has lost their way. James started his letter with the Sermon on the Mount, saying 'blessed is the one who endures temptation,' and he closes it with the same spirit, saying 'we count as blessed those who have persevered.' Between those two bookends lies a letter that refuses to let us separate our Sunday faith from our Monday life. May the Lord give us faith that endures, tongues that heal, wisdom that comes from above, hearts that are humble before Him, and the courage to live every day as the vapor that it is, filled with purpose, with gratitude, and with the unshakable hope that the Judge who stands at the door is also our Redeemer, the one who has already declared us not guilty through the precious blood of Christ.
